design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore interconnected overworld areas and dungeons, defeat enemies for experience and gold, purchase equipment and magic from shopkeepers, gain ranks from Gurus to unlock progression, solve environmental puzzles to access new areas
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (gold for equipment/magic) vs. enemy difficulty; navigation hazards (ladders, jumps) vs. combat threats; story revelation (poisoned water, transformed Dwarves) driving urgency
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Wanderer returning home to save corrupted homeland; wielding bladed weapons, magic projectiles, and armor upgrades; uncovering the mystery of The Evil One and restoring harmony between Elves and Dwarves
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Hybrid action-RPG treating platforming and combat with equal weight; screen-by-screen exploration over seamless scrolling; password/mantra system as save mechanism reflects religious/meditative theme; narrative integration of character rank and experience directly tied to NPC progression gating; side-scrolling perspective for story-driven action-RPG subverts top-down convention

difficulty.lives_system: Health bar (life bar) tracked on-screen; defeated by enemies when depleted; bread drops restore life during combat
difficulty.continues_system: Password system (called 'mantras' in international versions, 'prayers' in Japanese): obtained from church Gurus; preserves hero rank and experience totals upon resumption
difficulty.checkpointing: Screen-by-screen progression with Guru-dispensed passwords as checkpoint mechanism; no mid-dungeon saves documented
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: player can grind for gold to purchase better equipment and magic before tackling challenging areas; can choose exploration order within overworld structure; magic bar management adds resource dimension to combat difficulty

progression.upgrade_system: Equipment progression: bladed weapons, armor sets (Battle Helmet, Battle Suit unlock-gated behind Dragon Slayer acquisition); magic spells with individual powers (Thunder, others); consumable items (bread restores life); rank system tied to experience thresholds awarded by Gurus
progression.unlock_mechanism: Experience rank gates access to equipment and Guru blessings; keys gate dungeon progression; specific items (Ring of Dworf, Demon's Ring) unlock fortress entrances; Dragon Slayer acquisition required for Battle Helmet/Battle Suit equipping; suggests progression through four overworld areas: tree buttress, trunk interior, branches, Dwarves' mountain stronghold
replayability_hooks: Password system enables resume at any rank/experience state, supporting multiple playthroughs at different power levels
Character name registration (Japanese version only) personalizes journey
Unused dialog suggesting early design had optional/alternative dialogue branches based on inventory
Hidden/unused items in ROM (five total) suggest developer-intended discovery mechanics

world_building_techniques: NPC dialogue: townsfolk exposition on Dwarf invasion, water poisoning, World Tree lore; Gurus provide narrative beats tied to rank progression
Environmental storytelling: Eolis townscape in disrepair signals conflict; church architecture (crosses, crucifixes, rosaries in Japanese version) establish world spirituality
Inventory-tied dialogue: unused text shows magic type (Thunder) or equipment (Dragon Slayer) triggered conditional NPC responses, deepening story integration
Overworld structure: journey through tree buttress → trunk → branches → mountain stronghold mirrors narrative escalation toward Evil One confrontation

Character backstory: wanderer returning home after absence; Dwarf King Grieve swallowed magical sword to prevent Evil One's acquisition—mythology integrated into gameplay (sword recovery required for final boss)
Title/password terminology (mantras/prayers in Japanese, mantras in English) reinforce meditative/spiritual aesthetic

boss_design.philosophy: Boss encounters gate story progression and test learned combat patterns; Dwarf King Grieve encounter designed as multi-screen arena (unused extended room suggests original design spanned multiple screens); final confrontation against Evil One requires specific weapon (Dragon Slayer) obtained mid-game, enforcing narrative dependency
notable_bosses: Dwarf King Grieve: primary antagonist; multi-screen engagement hinted by unused extended boss room; swallowed Dragon Slayer sword to hide it from Evil One—sword recovery essential for victory
The Evil One: final boss; emerged from fallen meteorite; transformed Dwarves into monsters; requires Dragon Slayer to defeat; represents culmination of narrative arc

faction_indicators: Elves: allied with protagonist; Elven King provides initial quest and funding (1500 gold); fountain water poisoning affects their survival
Dwarves: transformed into hostile monsters by The Evil One against their will; Dwarf King Grieve attempts resistance by hiding Dragon Slayer; represents corrupted faction with sympathetic backstory
Humans/Wanderers: protagonist identity; returning from exile; neutral mediator position in Elf-Dwarf conflict
danger_signals: Poisoned water supply: environmental hazard signaling systemic corruption spreading through world
Dwarf hostility in Eolis: immediate threat establishing enemy presence and invasion stakes
Meteorite origin of The Evil One: cosmic threat emphasizing external corruption rather than internal conflict

Mandatory magic use requirement in final Dwarf fight (unused dialog: 'Don't rely on your magic power alone during the last fight against the Dwarfs'): hints at tactical complexity and multi-phase engagement requiring both melee and magic
Guru gating of progression: dependency on spiritual authority suggests societal hierarchy and limited agency without institutional blessing
environmental_storytelling: Eolis abandoned state: visual indicator of conflict impact and urgency of hero's return

Church architecture and clergy presence: establishes spiritual infrastructure and meditative tone; religious imagery differences (crosses, crucifixes removed in international versions) reflect localization priorities
World Tree structure: central mythological element; buttress→trunk→branches→stronghold geography mirrors descent into corruption and final confrontation
Shop NPCs and transaction mechanics: economy-driven progression emphasizes mercantile world rather than pure combat advancement

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor): Faxanadu page—unused content, regional differences, development remnants
wikipedia: Faxanadu—story, gameplay mechanics, development, release history, reception
